docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to FloofOS! This section guides you through initial setup and installation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS is a high-performance network operating system built on VPP (Vector Packet Processing) with BIRD2 for BGP routing.

docs.title: Quick Start Path
docs.text:
1. First Boot - Initial login and interface configuration
  2. Installation - Install FloofOS to permanent storage
  3. Quick Start - Basic configuration guide

docs.title: Default Credentials
docs.text:
Username Password Access root floofos Console only floofos floofos Console and SSH

Warning

Change default passwords immediately after first login.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text: FloofOS supports a wide range of hardware with network interfaces from 1G to 400G. The system auto-detects available NICs during first boot.

docs.title: Supported Interface Speeds
docs.text: Speed VPP Naming LCP Naming 1 Gbps GEX/Y/Z ge-x-y-z 10 Gbps 10GEX/Y/Z 10ge-x-y-z 25 Gbps 25GEX/Y/Z 25ge-x-y-z 40 Gbps 40GEX/Y/Z 40ge-x-y-z 100 Gbps 100GEX/Y/Z 100ge-x-y-z 400 Gbps 400GEX/Y/Z 400ge-x-y-z

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After completing installation:

  • Configure System Settings
  • Set Up User Accounts
  • Configure BGP
  • Enable Security Features

docs.title: First Boot Configuration
docs.text: This section describes the initial configuration process when booting FloofOS for the first time.

docs.title: Login Banner
docs.text:
Upon first boot, the system displays the following login prompt:

FloofOS Network Operating Sytem

floofos (tty1)
floofos login:

docs.title: Default Credentials
docs.text:
FloofOS ships with two default user accounts:

Username Password Access Method root floofos Console only (SSH disabled by default) floofos floofos Console and SSH

Security Notice

Change default passwords immediately after first login using the create user command. See User Management for details.

docs.title: Interface Detection Wizard
docs.text: When no VPP interface configuration exists, FloofOS automatically detects available network interfaces and presents a configuration wizard.

docs.title: Welcome Banner
docs.text:
After successful login, the system displays:

   ______          ___ ____  ____
  / __/ /__  ___  / _// __ \/ __/
 / _// / _ \/ _ \/ _// /_/ /\ \  
/_/ /_/\___/\___/_/  \____/___/  

Network Operating System
Copyright (c) 2026 | https://floofos.io

Welcome floofos (SSH from 172.16.32.1)
Sessions: 3 active | Last login: Thu Jan 15 18:34:06

Type '?' for available commands

docs.title: Interface Discovery
docs.text:
The wizard probes for available network interfaces:

Waiting for VPP...
Detected 1 network interface(s):

  VPP Name       Linux Name       Speed   NUMA   PCI Address
  ────────────   ──────────────   ─────   ────   ────────────────
  GE12/0/0       ge-12-0-0        1G      0      0000:00:12.0

Configure interfaces? [Y/n]:

docs.title: Interface Naming Convention
docs.text: FloofOS uses a structured naming scheme for network interfaces based on speed, slot position, and port index.

docs.title: VPP and Linux Control Plane (LCP) Names
docs.text: Speed VPP Interface LCP Interface (Linux) 1 Gbps GEX/Y/Z ge-x-y-z 2.5 Gbps 2.5GEX/Y/Z 2.5ge-x-y-z 10 Gbps 10GEX/Y/Z 10ge-x-y-z 25 Gbps 25GEX/Y/Z 25ge-x-y-z 40 Gbps 40GEX/Y/Z 40ge-x-y-z 50 Gbps 50GEX/Y/Z 50ge-x-y-z 100 Gbps 100GEX/Y/Z 100ge-x-y-z 400 Gbps 400GEX/Y/Z 400ge-x-y-z

docs.title: Naming Components
docs.text: Component Description X Card/Slot ID - Identifies the physical NIC or riser slot position Y NUMA Node - Indicates NUMA affinity (0 or 1 for dual-socket systems) Z Port Index - Sequential port number on the NIC

docs.title: Automatic Configuration (Recommended)
docs.text:
When prompted, enter Y to automatically create Linux Control Plane (LCP) interfaces:

Configure interfaces? [Y/n]: y

Configuring GE12/0/0 -> ge-12-0-0... done

Configured 1 interface(s)

Enter 'configure' mode and type 'commit' to save configuration

docs.title: Saving Configuration
docs.text:
After automatic configuration, enter configuration mode and commit changes:

floofos> conf
Entering configuration mode
floofos(config)# commit
Validating configuration...
Configuration committed successfully
floofos(config)#

docs.title: Manual Configuration
docs.text:
Enter N at the wizard prompt to skip automatic LCP creation. You must then manually create LCP mappings using VPP commands.

Recommendation

Automatic configuration is recommended for most deployments. Manual configuration provides granular control for advanced use cases.

docs.title: Verifying Configuration
docs.text:
After committing, verify the interface configuration:

floofos(config)# show configuration

The output displays the current running configuration including the newly created LCP interfaces.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After completing first boot configuration:

  1. Install to Disk - Install FloofOS to permanent storage
  2. Change Default Passwords - User Management
  3. Configure Hostname - System Configuration
  4. Set Up Network - Interface Configuration

docs.title: Installing FloofOS to Disk
docs.text: This section describes how to install FloofOS from installation media to permanent storage.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• FloofOS booted from installation media (USB, ISO, etc.)
  • Target disk for installation
  • Console or SSH access to the system

docs.title: Initiating Installation
docs.text:
From configuration mode, execute the installation command:

floofos(config)# system install 

FloofOS System Installation

This command will install FloofOS to permanent storage.
All existing data on the target disk will be erased.

Would you like to continue? [y/N]: y

docs.title: Disk Selection
docs.text:
The installer probes available disks and presents a selection menu:

Probing disks...
1 disk(s) found

The following disks were found:
  1: Drive: /dev/sda (32G) HARDDISK

Which disk should be used for installation? (Default: /dev/sda):

Data Loss Warning

Installation erases all data on the selected disk. Ensure you have backups of any important data.

docs.title: Installation Options
docs.text:
The installer prompts for configuration options:

Installation will delete all data on the drive. Continue? [y/N]: y

Would you like to use all the free space on the drive? [Y/n]: y

What console should be used by default? (K: KVM, S: Serial) [K]: 

What is the hostname of this system? [floofos]: core-router

Please enter a password for the 'floofos' user: 
Please confirm the password:

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Option Description Default Disk space Use all available space Yes Console Primary console type (KVM or Serial) KVM Hostname System hostname floofos Password Password for floofos user Required

docs.title: Configuration Preservation
docs.text:
If an existing configuration exists, the installer offers to preserve it:

Configuration found in floofos-config:

  1: commit 0

Would you like to preserve configuration? [Y/n]: y
Using configuration: commit 0

docs.title: Installation Summary
docs.text:
Before proceeding, the installer displays a summary:

Installation summary:

  Target disk     : /dev/sda
  Hostname        : core-router
  Console         : kvm
  User            : floofos
  Config restore  : commit 0

Proceed with installation? [y/N]: y

docs.title: Installation Progress
docs.text:
The installation proceeds through nine phases:

[1/9] Unmounting existing partitions...
[2/9] Creating partition table...
[3/9] Formatting partitions...
[4/9] Mounting target filesystem...
[5/9] Copying system files...
[6/9] Configuring system...
[7/9] Preserving configuration...
[8/9] Installing bootloader...
[9/9] Cleaning up...

Installation Time

Installation typically takes approximately 15 minutes depending on hardware performance.

docs.title: Completion Message
docs.text:
Upon successful installation:

FloofOS installation on disk completed successfully

Remove the installation media and reboot the system
To reboot now, enter configuration mode 'configure' and type 'system reboot'

docs.title: Rebooting the System
docs.text:
1. Remove the installation media (USB drive, etc.)
  2. Reboot the system:
floofos(config)# system reboot 
Reboot system? (Y/N): y

Manual Restart

If the system does not respond to the reboot command, you may perform a physical power cycle by disconnecting and reconnecting power to the hardware.

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
After reboot, verify the installation:

  1. Log in with configured credentials
  2. Verify configuration was preserved:
floofos(config)# show configuration

The previously committed interface configuration should appear in the output.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After successful installation:

  1. Configure System Settings
  2. Set Up User Accounts
  3. Configure Network Interfaces
  4. Enable Security Features

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get FloofOS configured and operational in minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• FloofOS installed on hardware or VM
  • Console or SSH access
  • Network connectivity information

docs.title: Step 1: First Login
docs.text:
Login with default credentials:

floofos login: floofos
Password: floofos

docs.title: Step 2: Interface Configuration
docs.text:
Accept the first-boot interface wizard:

Configure interfaces? [Y/n]: y

Enter configuration mode and commit:

floofos> conf
floofos(config)# commit

docs.title: Step 3: Change Hostname
docs.text:
floofos(config)# set hostname core-router
floofos(config)# commit

docs.title: Step 4: Change Passwords
docs.text:
Change default user password:

core-router(config)# create user floofos password YourSecurePassword
core-router(config)# commit

Change root password:

core-router(config)# create user root password AnotherSecurePassword
core-router(config)# commit

docs.title: Step 5: Configure Interface IP
docs.text:
core-router(config)# set interface ip address GE12/0/0 192.0.2.1/24
core-router(config)# set interface state GE12/0/0 up
core-router(config)# commit

docs.title: Step 6: Set Timezone
docs.text:
core-router(config)# set system time-zone Europe/London
core-router(config)# commit

docs.title: Step 7: Enable Security
docs.text:
Enable firewall:

core-router(config)# set security firewall enable
core-router(config)# commit

Verify Fail2ban is active:

core-router(config)# show security fail2ban status

docs.title: Step 8: Configure BGP (Optional)
docs.text:
Open BGP configuration:

core-router(config)# set protocol bgp

Edit configuration, then save (Ctrl+Q) and commit.

docs.title: Step 9: Install to Disk (If Needed)
docs.text:
If running from installation media:

core-router(config)# system install

Follow the installation wizard prompts.

docs.title: Verification Commands
docs.text:
# System status
show system

# Resource usage
show resource

# Running configuration
show configuration

# Interface status
show interface

# Security status
show security

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: This section covers monitoring capabilities in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FloofOS provides tools for monitoring system performance and network traffic:

  • SNMP - Integration with external monitoring systems
  • Traffic Monitoring - Real-time interface traffic visualization
  • Logging - System and audit logging

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Topic Description SNMP SNMP agent configuration Traffic Monitoring Real-time traffic display Logging System logging ---

docs.title: SNMP
docs.text:
# Enable SNMP
set service snmp enable

# Configure SNMP
set service snmp community <string>
set service snmp location <location>
set service snmp contact <email>

# View status
show service snmp

docs.title: Traffic Monitoring
docs.text:
# Real-time traffic
show traffic interface <interface>

docs.title: System Logging
docs.text:
# View logs
show system logging
show system logging last <n>
show system logging user <username>
show system logging today

docs.title: System Logging
docs.text: This section covers system logging and audit trail functionality in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS maintains an audit log that records system events, commands executed, and configuration changes. Logging is enabled by default.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show system logging

Displays the last 50 log entries. Each entry includes a timestamp, entry type ([CMD], [INFO], or [COMMIT]), username, and the command or event description.

docs.title: Log Entry Types
docs.text: Type Description [CMD] Command executed by user [INFO] Informational event (login, service change) [COMMIT] Configuration commit

docs.title: Last N Entries
docs.text:
show system logging last <n>

Display the last N log entries.

docs.title: By User
docs.text:
show system logging user <username>

Filter logs to show only entries from a specific user.

docs.title: Configuration Changes Only
docs.text:
show system logging config

Display only configuration-related entries.

docs.title: Commit Logs Only
docs.text:
show system logging commit

Display only commit entries.

docs.title: Today's Entries
docs.text:
show system logging today

Display only log entries from today.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set system logging enable
set system logging disable

Warning

Disabling logging removes audit trail functionality. Not recommended for production environments.

docs.title: Configuration Verification
docs.text:
Logging status appears in the running configuration:

show configuration

Look for set system logging enable or set system logging disable in the output.

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description show system logging Display last 50 entries show system logging last <n> Display last N entries show system logging user <name> Filter by user show system logging config Configuration changes only show system logging commit Commit logs only show system logging today Today's entries set system logging enable Enable logging set system logging disable Disable logging

docs.title: SNMP Configuration
docs.text: This section covers Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) configuration in FloofOS for network monitoring integration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FloofOS includes an SNMP agent that exposes VPP interface statistics directly to monitoring systems. The SNMP service is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled.

VPP Interface Monitoring

The SNMP agent reads interface statistics directly from VPP (not Linux LCP interfaces). When configuring monitoring systems (Zabbix, LibreNMS, PRTG, Grafana, Cacti), interface names appear as VPP interface names.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show service snmp

Displays the state of the snmpd service and vpp-snmp-agent, and when enabled, the listening address and port (UDP 161).

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service snmp enable
commit

docs.title: Disabling SNMP
docs.text:
set service snmp disable
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service snmp community <string>

Sets the SNMPv2c community string for read-only access.

Parameter Description Default string Community string public

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set service snmp community floof123
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service snmp location <string>

Sets the sysLocation MIB value for device identification.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set service snmp location DC_1
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service snmp contact <string>

Sets the sysContact MIB value for administrative contact information.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set service snmp contact noc@company.com
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show service snmp config

Displays the active SNMP configuration including community strings and system contact.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show service snmp statistics

Displays SNMP protocol counters including incoming/outgoing packet counts, bad community name attempts, GetNext requests, and GetResponse packets sent.

docs.title: Key Statistics
docs.text: Counter Description snmpInPkts Total incoming SNMP packets snmpOutPkts Total outgoing SNMP packets snmpInBadCommunityNames Requests with incorrect community snmpInGetNexts SNMP GetNext requests snmpOutGetResponses SNMP GetResponse packets sent

docs.title: Services Overview
docs.text:
View all services including SNMP:

show service

Displays status and configuration for all active services (SSH and SNMP).

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description set service snmp enable Enable SNMP agent set service snmp disable Disable SNMP agent set service snmp community <str> Set community string set service snmp location <str> Set system location set service snmp contact <str> Set system contact show service snmp Display SNMP status show service snmp config Display SNMP configuration show service snmp statistics Display SNMP counters show service Display all services

docs.title: Integration with Monitoring Systems
docs.text:
When configuring monitoring platforms:

  1. Protocol: SNMPv2c
  2. Port: UDP 161
  3. Community: As configured (default: public)
  4. Interface Names: VPP format (e.g., GE12/0/0)

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text:
• Zabbix
  • LibreNMS
  • PRTG
  • Grafana
  • Cacti
  • Other SNMP-compatible monitoring solutions

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Change default community - Never use public in production
  2. Use firewall rules - Restrict SNMP access to monitoring servers
  3. Set location and contact - Helpful for inventory and alerting
  4. Monitor statistics - Check snmpInBadCommunityNames for unauthorized access attempts

docs.title: Traffic Monitoring
docs.text: This section covers real-time traffic monitoring for VPP interfaces in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FloofOS provides real-time traffic visualization directly from VPP interfaces. The traffic monitor displays a text-based graphical interface showing throughput statistics.

VPP Direct Monitoring

Traffic statistics are read directly from VPP interfaces, not Linux LCP interfaces. Use VPP interface names (from show interface) when monitoring traffic.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: show traffic interface <interface>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description interface VPP interface name (e.g., GE12/0/0)

docs.title: Example
docs.text: show traffic interface GE12/0/0

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command launches a text-based user interface (TUI) displaying real-time traffic graphs, similar to bandwidth monitoring tools. The display shows:

  • Incoming traffic (RX)
  • Outgoing traffic (TX)
  • Current throughput rates
  • Historical traffic graph

docs.title: Exiting
docs.text: Press Ctrl+C or q to exit the traffic monitoring interface.

docs.title: Finding Interface Names
docs.text:
Before monitoring traffic, identify available interfaces:

show interface

Use the VPP interface name (left column) with the traffic monitoring command.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: Scenario Command Monitor uplink traffic show traffic interface GE12/0/0 Verify BGP session activity Monitor peer-facing interface Troubleshoot throughput Real-time visualization during testing

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text: Command Description show interface List all interfaces show resource Display system resource usage ping Test connectivity traceroute Trace packet path

docs.title: Operations
docs.text: This section covers operational procedures for FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FloofOS provides comprehensive tools for system operations:

  • Commit and Rollback - Configuration management and versioning
  • Backup and Restore - Configuration preservation and recovery
  • Diagnostics - Network troubleshooting tools

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Topic Description Commit and Rollback Configuration versioning Backup and Restore Configuration backup Network Diagnostics Ping and traceroute

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text:
# Commit changes
commit
commit comment <description>

# View commit history
show system commit

# Rollback
rollback <0-49>
system reboot

docs.title: Backup Operations
docs.text:
# Export backup
backup export <name>

# View backups
show backups

# Import backup
backup import <name>

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text:
# Connectivity test
ping <ip>

# Route tracing
traceroute <ip>
traceroute <ip> asn

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text: This section covers configuration backup and restore operations in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS supports exporting and importing configuration backups as compressed archives (.tar.gz). Backups include all configuration files and can be transferred off-device for safekeeping.

docs.title: Backup Storage Location
docs.text:
By default, backups are stored in:

/etc/floofos-config/backups/

Backup files can be downloaded using SCP or SFTP for off-device storage.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: backup export <name>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description name Backup filename (without extension)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
backup export backup1

The backup is saved to the default location as backup1.tar.gz.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: backup export <name> path <path>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description name Backup filename (without extension) path Target directory path

docs.title: Example
docs.text: backup export backup2 path /etc

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show backups

Displays exported backup files (name, timestamp, and size) from the default backup directory, followed by the rollback commit history.

docs.title: Output Description
docs.text:
Section Description Location Default backup directory Backup list Name, timestamp, and size of each backup Rollback History Commit history for rollback operations

Backup Display

Backup files are displayed without the .tar.gz extension, but the actual files include this extension. Only backups in the default location (/etc/floofos-config/backups) are shown.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: backup import <name>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description name Backup filename (without extension)

docs.title: Example
docs.text: backup import backup1

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: backup import <name> path <path>

docs.title: Example
docs.text: backup import backup2 path /etc

docs.title: Post-Import Procedure
docs.text:
After importing a backup:

  1. Commit the imported configuration: 

    commit

  2. Reboot the system: 

    system reboot

Reboot Required

Configuration from imported backups requires a system reboot to take full effect.

docs.title: Downloading Backups (SCP/SFTP)
docs.text:
Use SCP or SFTP to download backups from the router:

# From remote machine
scp floofos@router:/etc/floofos-config/backups/backup1.tar.gz ./

docs.title: Uploading Backups
docs.text:
Upload previously exported backups:

# To default location
scp backup1.tar.gz floofos@router:/etc/floofos-config/backups/

# To custom location
scp backup1.tar.gz floofos@router:/tmp/

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description backup export <name> Export backup to default location backup export <name> path <path> Export backup to custom path backup import <name> Import backup from default location backup import <name> path <path> Import backup from custom path show backups Display available backups commit Commit imported configuration system reboot Reboot after import

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Regular backups - Create backups before and after major changes
  2. Off-device storage - Transfer backups to external storage
  3. Descriptive names - Use meaningful backup names (e.g., pre-ixp-migration)
  4. Verify backups - Check show backups after export
  5. Document custom paths - Record path if using non-default location
  6. Test restore procedure - Periodically verify backup integrity

docs.title: Commit and Rollback
docs.text: This section covers configuration commit and rollback operations in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS uses a commit-based configuration model. Changes made in configuration mode are staged until committed. The system maintains up to 50 commit points for rollback purposes.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: commit comment <comment>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description comment Descriptive label for the commit (no spaces, use dashes)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
commit comment set-bgp-to-IXP

Comment Format

Comments cannot contain spaces. Use dashes (-) to separate words (e.g., add-upstream-peer).

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show system commit

Displays a numbered list of the last 50 commit timestamps and optional comments, with 0 being the current (most recent) commit.

docs.title: History Fields
docs.text:
Field Description Index Commit point number (0 = current) Timestamp Date and time of commit (current) Indicates active configuration Comment Optional commit comment in quotes

Commit Retention

FloofOS retains up to 50 commit points. Older commits are automatically purged as new commits are made.

docs.title: Understanding Rollback
docs.text: Rollback restores configuration to a previous commit point. After rollback, you must commit and reboot for changes to take effect.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
rollback

or

rollback 0

Both commands are equivalent and rollback to the current configuration state.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: rollback <0-49>

docs.title: Example
docs.text: rollback 5

docs.title: Post-Rollback Procedure
docs.text:
After rollback:

  1. Review the loaded configuration: show configuration
  2. Commit the rollback: commit
  3. Reboot the system: system reboot
commit
system reboot

Reboot Required

VPP configuration changes from rollback require a system reboot to take effect. Hot reload is not currently supported for rollback operations.

docs.title: Alternative to Rollback
docs.text:
For minor changes, consider manually reverting specific settings using delete commands instead of full rollback:

Original Command Revert Command set hostname core-router set hostname floofos set service ssh port 2234 delete service ssh port set security firewall enable set security firewall disable

For BGP configuration changes, edit the configuration file directly using set protocol bgp.

This approach avoids the reboot requirement of full rollback.

docs.title: Viewing Backup and Commit History
docs.text:
The show backups command displays both exported backups and commit history:

show backups

Displays exported backup files (name, timestamp, size) and the full rollback commit history.

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description commit Commit staged configuration commit comment <text> Commit with descriptive comment show system commit Display commit history rollback Rollback to previous state rollback <n> Rollback to specific commit point show backups Display backups and commit history system reboot Reboot system (required after rollback)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use commit comments - Document significant changes for easier identification
  2. Review before rollback - Check show system commit to identify correct rollback point
  3. Test changes incrementally - Commit after each major change for granular rollback options
  4. Plan maintenance windows - Rollback requires reboot, plan accordingly
  5. Consider manual revert - For single changes, manual revert avoids reboot requirement

docs.title: Network Diagnostics
docs.text: This section covers network troubleshooting tools available in FloofOS, including ping and traceroute.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS provides built-in network diagnostic tools that operate directly through VPP. These commands can be executed from both operational mode and configuration mode.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The ping command sends ICMP echo requests to test connectivity. Ping is handled directly by VPP for accurate dataplane testing.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: ping {<ip-addr> | ipv4 <ip4-addr> | ipv6 <ip6-addr>} [ipv4 <ip4-addr> | ipv6 <ip6-addr>] [source <interface>] [size <pktsize:60>] [interval <sec:1>] [repeat <cnt:5>] [table-id <id:0>] [burst <count:1>] [verbose]

docs.title: Common Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Default source <interface> Source interface Auto size <pktsize> Packet size in bytes 60 interval <sec> Interval between pings 1 repeat <cnt> Number of pings 5 table-id <id> VRF/routing table ID 0 burst <count> Burst count 1 verbose Verbose output Off

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Basic ping
ping 172.16.32.1

# Ping from specific interface
ping 192.168.1.1 source GE1/0/0

# Custom count and size
ping 10.0.0.1 repeat 10 size 1400

docs.title: Error Messages
docs.text:
Message Meaning Failed: no egress interface No route to destination (missing gateway) Request timeout ICMP request timed out

No Egress Interface

If you receive "no egress interface" errors, verify that a default gateway is configured. This can be set via BGP or static routes in the global-config section of BGP configuration.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The traceroute command traces the path packets take to reach a destination.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: traceroute <address> [asn | source <ip> | interface <interface>]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description address Destination IP address or hostname asn Display ASN information for each hop source <ip> Source IP address interface <interface> Source interface

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Basic traceroute
traceroute 172.16.32.1

# Traceroute with ASN display
traceroute 1.1.1.1 asn

# Traceroute from specific source
traceroute 8.8.8.8 source 10.0.0.1

ASN Lookup Requirement

The asn option requires a working default route. Configure a gateway via BGP or static routes in the global-config section of BGP configuration.

docs.title: Connectivity Issues
docs.text: Symptom Possible Cause Resolution "no egress interface" No default route Configure BGP or static gateway "Network is unreachable" No route to network Verify routing configuration Request timeout Firewall/ACL blocking Check firewall rules High latency Network congestion Check interface traffic

docs.title: Verifying Routes
docs.text:
Before running diagnostics, verify routing:

show configuration

Look for: - Interface IP addresses - BGP peer configuration - Static routes in global-config

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description ping <ip> Basic connectivity test ping <ip> source <if> Ping from specific interface ping <ip> repeat <n> Ping with custom count traceroute <ip> Trace route to destination traceroute <ip> asn Trace with ASN display traceroute <ip> source <ip> Trace from specific source

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• BGP Configuration - Configure routing and gateways
  • Traffic Monitoring - Real-time traffic analysis
  • Interface Configuration - Interface setup

docs.title: Security
docs.text: This section covers security configuration in FloofOS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FloofOS provides multiple layers of security for protecting the control plane and managing access:

  • User Management - Account administration and authentication
  • SSH - Secure remote access configuration
  • Firewall - Control plane protection using nftables
  • Fail2ban - Brute-force attack prevention

docs.title: Default Security Posture
docs.text: Feature Default State Recommendation Firewall Disabled Enable in production Fail2ban Enabled Keep enabled Root SSH Disabled Keep disabled Default Passwords Set Change immediately

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Topic Description User Management Create, modify, and delete users SSH Configuration Secure shell settings Firewall nftables firewall rules Fail2ban Brute-force protection

docs.title: Essential Security Commands
docs.text:
# View security status
show security

# User management
show users
create user <name> password <pass>
delete user <name>

# Firewall
set security firewall enable
show security firewall rules

# Fail2ban
show security fail2ban status
show security fail2ban banned

docs.title: Fail2ban Configuration
docs.text: FloofOS includes Fail2ban for SSH brute-force protection. This section covers Fail2ban configuration and monitoring.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Fail2ban monitors authentication logs and temporarily bans IP addresses that show malicious signs, such as repeated failed login attempts. By default, Fail2ban is enabled to protect SSH access.

Security Recommendation

Keep Fail2ban enabled on all production systems. Additionally, change all default passwords immediately after deployment.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: set security fail2ban enable

docs.title: Disabling Fail2ban
docs.text: set security fail2ban disable

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show security fail2ban status

Displays Fail2ban state, max retry threshold, ban time, find time window, and current/total banned IP counts.

docs.title: Status Fields
docs.text: Field Description Default Fail2ban Service state enabled Max retry Failed attempts before ban 3 Ban time Ban duration in seconds 600 (10 minutes) Find time Time window for counting failures 600 (10 minutes) Currently banned Active bans - Total banned Cumulative bans since boot -

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show security fail2ban banned

Displays the SSH jail status including current/total failed attempts, monitored log files, and the list of currently banned IP addresses.

docs.title: Output Description
docs.text:
Section Description Filter Log parsing statistics Currently failed Active failed attempts in find window Total failed Cumulative failed attempts File list Monitored log files Actions Ban enforcement statistics Banned IP list Currently banned IP addresses

Note

When brute-force attempts occur, banned IP addresses appear in the "Banned IP list" field.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set security fail2ban maxretry <number>

Sets the number of failed authentication attempts allowed before an IP is banned.

Parameter Description Default number Maximum attempts before ban 3

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set security fail2ban bantime <seconds>

Sets how long an IP address remains banned.

Parameter Description Default seconds Ban duration in seconds 600

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description set security fail2ban enable Enable Fail2ban set security fail2ban disable Disable Fail2ban set security fail2ban maxretry <n> Set max failed attempts set security fail2ban bantime <sec> Set ban duration show security fail2ban status Display Fail2ban status show security fail2ban banned Display banned IPs show security Display all security status

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Keep Fail2ban enabled - Essential protection against brute-force attacks
  2. Change default passwords - Fail2ban is a safety net, not a replacement for strong credentials
  3. Use SSH keys - Configure SSH key authentication for additional security
  4. Monitor banned IPs - Regularly check show security fail2ban banned for attack patterns
  5. Adjust thresholds - Tune maxretry and bantime based on your security requirements

docs.title: Firewall Configuration
docs.text: FloofOS includes a stateful firewall based on nftables for control plane protection. This section covers firewall configuration and rule management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The firewall protects the control plane by filtering traffic destined to the router itself. By default, the firewall is disabled and must be explicitly enabled.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set security firewall enable
commit

docs.title: Disabling the Firewall
docs.text:
set security firewall disable
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show security firewall status

Displays firewall state, default chain policies, active rule count, last modified timestamp, and control plane protection status.

docs.title: Status Fields
docs.text: Field Description Firewall Current state (enabled/disabled) Policy Default chain policies Rules Active rule count and breakdown Last modified Timestamp of last rule change Control plane Protection status

docs.title: Default Rules
docs.text: When enabled, the firewall includes default rules for essential network services:

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show security firewall rules

Displays all active firewall rules with their name, protocol, source, destination port, and action.

docs.title: Default Rule Descriptions
docs.text:
Rule Name Protocol Port Purpose system-ssh TCP 22 Secure Shell access system-bgp TCP 179 BGP inbound sessions system-bgp-out TCP 179 (source) BGP outbound sessions system-bfd UDP 3784-3785 BFD inbound system-bfd-out UDP 3784-3785 (source) BFD outbound system-snmp UDP 161 SNMP queries

Note

Rules prefixed with system- are default FloofOS rules. These can be deleted and recreated as needed.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: set security firewall rule <name> protocol <tcp|udp> port <port> [src-address <ip/cidr>] action <accept|drop>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Required name Unique rule identifier Yes protocol Transport protocol (tcp/udp) Yes port Destination port or port range Yes src-address Source IP address with CIDR notation No action Rule action (accept/drop) Yes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set security firewall rule api-lookingglass protocol tcp port 5000 action accept
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: delete security firewall rule <name>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
delete security firewall rule system-snmp
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show security

Displays a combined status summary of both the firewall (state, policy, rule count) and Fail2ban (state, thresholds, ban counts).

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description set security firewall enable Enable firewall set security firewall disable Disable firewall show security firewall status Display firewall status show security firewall rules List all firewall rules set security firewall rule <name> ... Create firewall rule delete security firewall rule <name> Delete firewall rule show security Display all security status

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Enable firewall in production - Always enable the firewall on production systems
  2. Review default rules - Assess whether all default rules are necessary for your deployment
  3. Use specific source addresses - When possible, restrict rules by source IP/CIDR
  4. Document custom rules - Use descriptive names for custom rules
  5. Commit changes - Remember to commit after making firewall changes

docs.title: SSH Service Configuration
docs.text: This section covers SSH server configuration in FloofOS, including port settings, access control, and key management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: SSH is enabled by default on FloofOS for remote management. The service runs in the dataplane namespace and supports both password and public key authentication.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show service ssh

Displays the current SSH configuration including port, listen address, root login permission, password authentication state, public key authentication state, and service state.

docs.title: Status Fields
docs.text: Field Description Default Port SSH listening port 22 Listen Address Bound IP addresses 0.0.0.0, :: (all interfaces) Root Login Root SSH access permission disabled Password Auth Password authentication enabled Pubkey Auth Public key authentication enabled Service Service state active

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: set service ssh port <1-65535>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set service ssh port 2234
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: delete service ssh port

docs.title: Root Login Access
docs.text: By default, the root user cannot authenticate via SSH. This can be modified if required.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service ssh root-login enable
commit

docs.title: Disabling Root SSH Access
docs.text:
set service ssh root-login disable
commit

Security Consideration

Enabling root SSH login increases security risk. Consider using a regular administrative user with key-based authentication instead.

docs.title: Listen Address Binding
docs.text: Restrict SSH to specific IP addresses for enhanced security.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: set service ssh listen-address <ipv4/ipv6>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
set service ssh listen-address 172.16.32.1
commit

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: delete service ssh listen-address <ipv4/ipv6>

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
set service ssh key <username> <public-key>

Adds an SSH public key for key-based authentication. Use this when adding keys to existing users without changing their password.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
delete service ssh key <username>

Removes all SSH public keys for the specified user.

SSH Key Generation

Generate SSH key pairs using PuTTYgen (Windows) or ssh-keygen (Linux/macOS). Copy the complete public key string when configuring.

docs.title: Services Overview
docs.text:
View all services including SSH:

show service

Displays status and configuration for all active services (SSH and SNMP).

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Description show service ssh Display SSH configuration set service ssh port <port> Set SSH port delete service ssh port Reset SSH port to default set service ssh root-login enable Allow root SSH login set service ssh root-login disable Disallow root SSH login set service ssh listen-address <ip> Bind SSH to specific IP delete service ssh listen-address <ip> Reset to all interfaces set service ssh key <user> <key> Add SSH key for user delete service ssh key <user> Remove SSH keys for user

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use non-standard port - Consider changing from port 22 to reduce automated attacks
  2. Disable root login - Use regular administrative accounts
  3. Use SSH keys - Prefer key-based authentication over passwords
  4. Bind to management interface - Restrict SSH to specific management IP when possible
  5. Enable Fail2ban - Keep Fail2ban enabled for brute-force protection

docs.title: User Management
docs.text: This section covers user account administration in FloofOS, including creating, modifying, and deleting user accounts.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FloofOS provides a unified command interface for managing user accounts. All user management commands require configuration mode and must be committed to take effect.

docs.title: Default Users
docs.text:
FloofOS ships with two default accounts:

Username Default Password Access Notes root floofos Console only SSH disabled by default floofos floofos Console and SSH Primary administrative user

Security Best Practice

Change default passwords immediately after deployment.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
create user <username> password <password> [ssh-key <public-key>]
commit

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Required username Account username Yes password Account password Yes ssh-key SSH public key for key-based authentication No

docs.title: Example: Create New User
docs.text:
create user NOC password SecretPass123
commit

docs.title: Example: Create User with SSH Key
docs.text:
create user NOC password SecretPass123 ssh-key ssh-rsa AAAA...
commit

SSH Key Generation

SSH public keys can be generated using PuTTYgen or ssh-keygen. Paste the complete public key string when configuring.

docs.title: Root Password
docs.text:
create user root password YourPass123
commit

docs.title: FloofOS User Password
docs.text:
create user floofos password YourPass1234
commit

docs.title: Updating Existing User Password
docs.text:
To change an existing user's password, re-issue the create user command with the new password:

create user NOC password NewPass123
commit

Password Updates

The create user command automatically detects existing users and updates their credentials instead of creating duplicates. This same method applies to updating SSH keys.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
delete user <username>
commit

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
delete user NOC
commit

docs.title: Restrictions
docs.text:
• The root user cannot be deleted
  • All other users, including the default floofos user, can be deleted

Caution

Ensure at least one administrative user with SSH access remains before deleting the floofos user.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
show users

Displays all FloofCTL user accounts with their username, privilege level, and creation timestamp. Built-in system accounts are marked as (system).

docs.title: Output Fields
docs.text: Field Description Username Account name Privilege User privilege level (admin) Created Account creation timestamp or (system) for built-in accounts

docs.title: SSH Key Management
docs.text: For managing SSH keys on existing users without changing passwords, see SSH Service Configuration.

docs.title: Adding SSH Key to Existing User
docs.text: set service ssh key <username> <public-key>

docs.title: Removing SSH Key from User
docs.text: delete service ssh key <username>
